experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] contains a known evil player, so from Good's perspective this team is highly likely to fail and should be rejected. As Merlin, rejecting avoids sending a bad team while not revealing too much beyond normal good play; approving would help evil gain control early.
Player 2: No concrete role information yet from a first-round proposal; players 0 and 3 are largely unknown. As a Good Servant on team voting in round 1, approving a reasonable 2-player team is typically optimal to gather information and avoid needless early rejections. My vote reveals little beyond a standard pro-information stance and does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 1, 2, and 3 are good. Proposed team [0,3] includes me and one good player. In round 1 as evil, approving a team with myself on it is strong because I can choose to fail the quest and put evil ahead immediately. My vote reveals little since many players approve early teams, and rejecting a team containing myself would be suspicious and unhelpful.
Player 4: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; player 3 selected a team including 0, which gives evil a chance to control quest 1. As Minion on team voting, optimal evil play is usually to approve teams containing an evil player, especially early when a fail can create confusion and secure an early point. My vote itself reveals little because approving a plausible round-1 team is common for either alignment.
Player 3: I only know my own role: I am Good. On round 1 with no prior information, a team containing myself is generally acceptable, and as proposer I selected [3,0], which is a standard low-information opener. Rejecting my own first-round team would create unnecessary suspicion and provide little benefit. Approving reveals little beyond consistency and helps progress the game.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know only that I am Good and on the first quest team with player 0; there is very limited role information so far besides player 1 rejecting this team. In quest voting, as a Servant, optimal play is always to support the quest with a pass. Voting pass reveals nothing harmful and is required to help Good succeed.
Player 0: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 1, 2, and 3 are nominally good/unknown, with 3 having proposed this 2-person team including me. On quest 1 as Assassin, the optimal play is usually to avoid an immediate fail on a 2-person team because a round-1 fail with me on it creates very strong suspicion on either 0 or 3 and can isolate me early. Passing keeps my cover, especially since only one reject came from player 1, which gives evil more room to shape later quests. My action reveals less if I pass now; an early fail gives information to good, while a pass preserves ambiguity and helps evil in the long game.

On quest 2 with team size 3, a single fail will make the quest fail, so approving is strongly favorable for evil. experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil, so as Good I should reject to prevent an evil-controlled quest team. Rejecting also avoids signaling too strongly beyond normal good play, since any reasonable player should oppose a team including 0 after the prior failed mission with 0 and 4 both on it.
Player 2: Player 0 looks somewhat good from Quest 1 success with 3, but Quest 2 [0,1,4] had 2 fails, which strongly implies both 1 and 4 are evil and clears 0 as good. As a known good servant, I should favor teams excluding 1 and 4. Proposed team [0,2] is optimal for a 2-person quest given current info. Approving also signals support for the likely-good pair and helps Good avoid wasting rounds.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] got 2 fails, confirming both 0 and 4 were on it and we successfully sabotaged. Current proposed team [0,2] includes me but not 4. As evil on a 2-person quest, if approved I can fail it alone and put evil up 2-1, which is very strong. Voting to approve is optimal. Approving may look slightly suspicious since I proposed it, but the strategic value of getting myself onto the quest outweighs that.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [3,0] succeeded, which could be because 0 chose to pass. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] failed with 2 fails, strongly outing 0 and me to observant players, so team [0,2] is highly suspicious from a good perspective. As evil, my goal is to get evil-containing teams approved and avoid looking too coordinated. Voting approve helps push through a team with 0 on it; if it passes, 0 can fail quest 3 for an evil lead. My approve may look self-serving, but rejecting an evil team hurts our win chances more.
Player 3: I know team [0,1,4] produced 2 fails, so 0/1/4 contains at least two evil; player 2 was not on that failed mission and my prior team [3,0] succeeded, making 2 look relatively better than 1 or 4, while 0 is mixed but somewhat suspicious from being on the double-fail team. As Good on a team-voting phase, I should reject teams that include likely evil when there are safer alternatives; [0,2] still includes suspicious 0 and excludes me, and approving would give evil another strong chance to fail quest 3. My vote signals skepticism of 0 after the double-fail evidence, which helps good coordinate around avoiding 0/1/4-heavy teams.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils, so it is the strongest available team and should be approved. Approving signals confidence in a clean team, which helps good without exposing too much since Merlin should still support obviously good compositions.
Player 0: I am evil Assassin with teammate 4. Team [1,2,3] excludes both evils, so if approved it should succeed, giving Good a second success and making score 2-2 before quest 5. We already have 2 evil quest wins; as evil, I want to prevent a clean good team from passing if possible. Rejecting is optimal and consistent with suspicion that at least some of 1,2,3 are good, since prior fails came from teams containing 0 and/or 4. My vote reveals some opposition to a no-evil team, but that's acceptable because evil benefits from blocking this quest.
Player 4: I know 0 is evil with me. Team [0,1,4] got 2 fails, strongly implicating 1 as evil or at least confirms 0+4 were both on it and both failed. Team [0,2] got 1 fail, consistent with 0 evil and 2 likely good. Team [3,0] succeeded, which can happen if 0 chose pass to hide. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils 0 and 4, so if 1 were actually good this team is all good and would likely succeed, which is bad for evil while we already lead 2-1. As evil, optimal play is to reject good-looking teams and preserve chances to get an evil onto quest 4. My reject signals some suspicion but that is acceptable and aligned with evil interests.
Player 2: I know I am Good. Quest 2 team [0,1,4] had 2 fails, so at least two of {0,1,4} are Evil. Quest 3 team [0,2] had 1 fail, and since I am Good, player 0 must be Evil. Therefore on Quest 2, at least one of {1,4} is also Evil. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes confirmed Evil 0, but includes 1 who is suspicious from the double-fail quest; 3 was on the successful first quest and looks better. At 1 Good-2 Evil on Quest 4, approving a team with a likely Evil is very risky because a fail loses the game for Good. Rejecting preserves a chance to find a safer team. My vote signals suspicion on 1 and consistency with the deductions from prior quests, which helps Good more than approving a risky team.
Player 3: I am Good (Servant). From quest history: Quest 2 team [0,1,4] had 2 fails, so among 0/1/4 at least two are Evil. Quest 3 team [0,2] had 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 2 is Evil. Since [0,1,4] contains at least two Evils, 2 is more likely Good than 0. Also my Quest 1 team [3,0] succeeded, which does not clear 0 because Evil can pass early, but it does clear me as Good from my perspective. Proposed team [1,2,3] includes 1, who is highly suspicious from the double-fail team, and excludes 4, but still likely contains an Evil. As Good on vote phase with Evil already at 2 quest wins, I should reject teams that are likely compromised. Rejecting reveals I am resistant to suspicious compositions and helps Good avoid an immediate likely loss.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate; 1 and 2 were on the last rejected good-looking team [1,2,3], and now 2 proposes [1,2,4] including my teammate 4. With evil already at 2 quest wins, approving a team with 4 gives us a direct chance to secure the 3rd fail and win before assassination matters. My vote reveals little since evil may plausibly support or oppose, but approval maximizes immediate win chances.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,4] includes known evil 4, so it should be rejected. We are down 1-2 and this is quest 4, so approving a team with a known evil likely loses immediately. Rejecting preserves a chance to get a clean team and does not reveal more than necessary, since rejecting a team with 4 is consistent with good play.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Known data: Quest 1 [0,1,4] succeeded, so at most one of 0/1/4 is evil on a 3-player quest, but since it had 0 fails all three are somewhat better than average. Quest 2 [0,2] failed with 1 fail, and I am good, so 0 must be evil. Quest 3 proposed [1,2,3] was rejected heavily, giving limited info. Current proposed team [1,2,4] excludes confirmed evil 0 and includes me plus two players from the clean first quest. For Good, approving is optimal because this is a strong team and we must win the next two quests. My vote reveals I trust the strongest available non-0 lineup, which helps Good coordination more than it helps Evil.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 team [0,1,4] succeeded, so likely those players are cleaner than 2, though one evil could still hide there. Quest 2 team [0,2] failed with 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 2 is evil. Since 0 was on the successful first quest and 2 has now been on both failed quests, 2 is highly suspicious. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes 2, making it very risky on quest 4 when Evil already has 2 points. As Good in team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing the strongest evil suspect. My vote signals suspicion on 2, which helps Good coordinate.
Player 4: I am Minion with evil teammate 0. Team [1,2,4] includes me but not 0. From prior quests: [0,1,4] got 2 fails, confirming 0 and me were both on it; [0,2] got 1 fail, consistent with 0 failing and 2 likely good. So 1 and 2 are probably good, making this team have only one evil (me). On quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, any failed quest wins for evil, and a single evil on a 3-person team can fail it. Approving is optimal. My approval is not too revealing because as a team member I often approve, and evil wants this team to go.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and good, so I must vote pass on quests. Known evil includes 4, and this team [1,2,4] contains an evil player, so the quest may fail from their sabotage, but my optimal play is still to pass. Failing as good is illegal/suboptimal and would only harm good while revealing nothing useful.
Player 2: I am Good Servant on the current quest team, so I must vote pass. From prior results, team [0,1,4] had 2 fails, strongly suggesting 0/1/4 contains both evils; team [0,2] had 1 fail, and since I am good that implicates 0. That makes 1 and 4 the main candidates for the second evil. Regardless, as Good my optimal quest action is always pass; failing would only hurt my team and falsely signal evil behavior.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [0,1,4] had 2 fails, confirming both 0 and I failed there; this likely makes 1 look somewhat better. Quest 2 team [0,2] had 1 fail, likely from 0, which can cast suspicion on 2 as well. Now on quest 4, evil already has 2 quest fails total, so one more fail wins immediately. As Minion on the team, optimal play is to sabotage now. The information revealed is just that at least one of [1,2,4] is evil, but ending the game by quest wins outweighs any deduction risk.
